data.title: 3i Group: Cyclical Slowdown Doesn't Change Long-Term Growth Story
data.description:
As I covered about one year ago, I’m bullish on 3i Group plc (TGOPY) as the company’s stake in Action should drive growth for many years ahead. However, its share price has been quite weak since then, dropping by more than 30% and underperforming the overall stock market (SPY) by a wide margin, reflecting cyclical issues rather than some fundamental concerns, in my opinion.

Indeed, this weak share price performance is justified by a de-rating of its shares due to slower growth at its main asset, Action, impacted by softer consumer trends over the past few months across Europe. This has resulted in lower like-for-like (LfL) sales at Action, increasing by 4.9% YoY during 2025 compared to 10.3% YoY in the previous year.

This weaker result was driven especially by France, which was a clear laggard to LfL sales growth and generates about one-third of Action’s revenues. LfL sales in France were even negative during a couple of months over the last quarter of 2025, while they have rebounded to positive territory in the last few weeks of 2025 and during the beginning of 2026. However, considering recent macroeconomic headwinds related to higher fuel prices and the negative impact this has on consumer spending, it’s quite likely that LfL sales growth will remain relatively low during the rest of 2026 even though they have recovered to 4% in the first twelve weeks of 2026 (vs. 1.9% in Q4 2025), being a negative factor for 3i Group’s valuation in the short term.

Indeed, while 3i Group was trading at about 1.4x Net Asset Value (NAV) when I last analyzed it back in May 2025, its shares are now trading at a slight discount to NAV. This shows that 3i Group’s shares have de-rated quite significantly over the past few months, leading to its stock trading nowadays at a discount to its historical patterns.

Using the price-to-book value as a proxy for valuation, 3i Group is currently trading at about 0.94x book value, which is at a discount to its own history over the last decade and is a much lower multiple than some months ago, as can be seen in the next graph. This shows that investor sentiment has shifted quite aggressively in the recent past, which is not particularly surprising for a stock that is largely exposed to the consumer sector that is naturally cyclical and vulnerable to economic cycles.

As can be seen in the previous graph, compared to its history over the last decade, 3i Group has traded consistently above book value, even though there have been brief periods when its valuation went below book value, such as during COVID in 2020 or the bear market in 2022. However, these periods seem to be good buying opportunities for long-term investors, as 3i Group eventually returns to a ‘normal’ valuation above book value, supported by its strong fundamentals and growth prospects.

While, from a cyclical perspective, Action’s sales growth has decelerated in recent months, the company’s expansion plans maintained their strong path during last year. Action achieved net sales of €16 billion in 2025 and EBITDA of €2.37 billion, up by 16% and 14%, respectively. This was supported by its expansion efforts, namely by opening 384 net new stores during the year, representing an increase of 13% YoY and representing a new annual record of openings. This is also above the number of new stores I was expecting when I covered 3i Group last year, as I was expecting about 370 stores to open during 2025.

While these are still impressive growth rates, they're lower compared to its long-term history, given that Action has increased sales and EBITDA by 22% and 31% CAGR over the past few years, respectively. Additionally, Action has also been able to consistently increase its EBITDA margin in the past due to operating efficiencies as the business gets larger, but this changed during 2025 as the EBITDA margin declined by 30 basis points (bps) to 14.8%, being the first annual decline since 2019, due to higher supply chain costs and a one-off staff payment related to the 3,000 stores milestone.

Nevertheless, even though its operating performance has been weaker in recent months, Action continues to outperform its peers, reporting higher stores, sales, and earnings growth than its competitors, showing that its business model is superior in the European hard discount retail segment compared to peers.

Having said that, as can be seen from the previous graph, Action’s growth has decelerated more recently closer to its peer’s average rate, which is not surprising considering its increased size and larger footprint, making it more difficult to grow revenue and earnings at +20% per year going forward.

Despite that, Action’s growth prospects remain very strong, as the company still has plenty of room for further penetration in its existing markets and continues to expand its business to new countries. During 2025, Action entered Switzerland and Romania, opening eight stores in Switzerland and six in Romania, while it added three new distribution centers in Germany, Poland, and Italy to further support store openings in these regions. At the end of 2025, Action had more than 3,330 stores and fifteen distribution centers across Continental Europe, operating in fourteen European countries. For 2026, Action expects to enter Croatia and Slovenia, while Bulgaria should be added in 2027, and the U.S. is expected to be a new region in late 2027 or early 2028.

While Action has very good growth prospects in Europe and can easily, in my opinion, maintain double-digit growth rates over the next decade operating new stores just on this continent, I think its entry into the U.S. market will be an important milestone and can add another important layer of further business growth over the long term. Obviously, there is considerable risk in entering a new geography from scratch, and the required investments are significant, but if Action is successful in rolling out its operations in the U.S., this could be a boost to its long-term growth prospects in a significant way.

The U.S. discount market is quite competitive and dominated by two dollar-store chains, namely Dollar General Corporation (DG) and Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR), but there are some success stories from European retailers in the U.S. (like Lidl and Aldi), which bodes well for a well-run European retailer like Action to successfully enter the U.S. market. Action plans to have about 100 stores in the U.S. by 2030, which means the company is not planning a rapid rollout of operations in the country, which reduces risk and makes the investment not demanding from a financial perspective, as capex needs will be manageable.

Action is planning to enter the U.S. market in the Southeastern area, where competition is intense, making good execution of its operational rollout critical for the long-term success of its operations in the country. Action is not expected to change much of its business model of offering own-brand products, a same-store concept, and pricing leadership, but entering a new geography is always risky, as consumer habits are different from Europe and the scale required to operate efficiently in the U.S. is much higher than compared to Europe.

Given this backdrop, I think 3i Group is likely to maintain strong NAV growth ahead, driven largely by its main asset, Action. For 2026, Action plans to open at least 400 new stores (+12% YoY) across existing and new countries, which should support double-digit revenue and earnings growth.

During the first nine months of fiscal year (FY) 2026, 3i Group’s total return was 20%, leading to a NAV above £30 per share at the end of 2025, up by 18.5% compared to its NAV at the end of FY 2025. This means 3i Group is currently trading at only 0.88x NAV, which seems quite undemanding and leads to upside potential both from business growth ahead and also a potential valuation multiple recovery in the future if operating conditions improve over the coming quarters. Investors should note that 3i Group’s fiscal year ends in March, and the company will report its annual earnings next May 14, which will be important to see if operating momentum has recovered during the last quarter and what the management is expecting over the next few quarters.

Conclusion

While 3i Group’s growth has decelerated recently due to some cyclical headwinds at Action, namely in France, where its operating performance has been weaker in recent months, its long-term growth prospects remain quite good, and the company continues to execute well on its expansion plans across Europe. Moreover, the entry into the U.S. may add an important growth driver over the long term if the company can replicate its European success, even though there is obviously execution risk and a successful rollout is not certain. Considering this backdrop, I see 3i Group’s valuation as quite cheap and move my recommendation to ‘Strong Buy,’ as the long-term story remains quite compelling in my opinion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Right Play On Salesforce
data.description:
Shares of enterprise software behemoth Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) have declined just over 50% since putting in an all-time high in December 2024 as its subscription-based model comes under AI attack. Management has a much different take, expecting the company to grow its top line at an 11% CAGR through the end of the decade, while ambitiously projecting achieving Rule of 50 in FY30. Floating $25 billion of debt on March 11, 2026, with a sole purpose of executing on its $50 billion repurchase program (30% of stock outstanding), the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

Salesforce, Inc. is a San Francisco based enterprise software platform providing customer relationship management technology featuring sales, service, marketing, commerce, collaboration, data management, integration, analytics, IT service, and vertical-specific offerings. The company services over 150,000 businesses that pay for anywhere from one to hundreds of thousands of subscriptions for its core offerings. Salesforce was founded in 1999 and went public in 2004, raising net proceeds of $113.8 million at $2.75 per share, after giving effect to a 4-for-1 stock split in 2013. Shares of CRM trade around $175.00 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of just over $140 billion.

The company operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending January 31st. For the avoidance of doubt, the 12 months ending January 31, 2026 is FY26.

Shift from Subscription to Consumption Models in the Age of AI

Its subscription-based products (data, sales, service, marketing, analytics, etc.) have been somewhat stitched together from an incessant acquisition spree, with the company having onboarded ~75 partners since inception. In a race to maintain relevancy in the software-as-a-service (SaaS) space, Salesforce is integrating AI agents into all of its offerings. The prime example of this evolution is its Agentforce offerings, which allow customers to build and deploy an army of around-the-clock digital laborers that employees and customers can leverage to increase productivity. Businesses using Agentforce Service can onboard customer, employee, IT, and field service needs onto one integrated, AI-powered platform to deliver bespoke customer support at scale. These agents are not performing simple record keeping or summary tasks — like a next gen search engine — but rather performing functions that require logic, such as billing resolution.

Adding to its functionality and suite of offerings was the company’s $9.6 billion acquisition of AI-powered enterprise cloud data management platform Informatica. Consummated in November 2025, customers can now deconstruct data silos, placing it all into Agentforce’s data engine Data 360 while maintaining integrity without compromising compliance or security controls.

Also a feature of Agentforce (and all Salesforce offerings) is Slack, which centralizes communications, automates business processes and knowledge sharing. It now features Slackbot, which (for lack of a better descriptor) functions as a personal secretary and consultant.

Since their introductions, Salesforce platforms have delivered 2.4 billion agentic transactions – or work units as management refers to them.

Share Price Performance

With Salesforce growing in leaps and bounds and SaaS concerns en vouge, its stock appreciated 13,318% from its IPO price in 2004 to $369.00 per share on December 4, 2024. Since then, shares of CRM are currently down 50%, having hit a three-year low in February 2026, as concerns that AI is going to extirpate software platforms like Salesforce’s. Having essentially created the enterprise SaaS subscription model, fears are that it will become the 2020s’ version of Lotus Notes.

Those concerns are overblown with a tinge of legitimacy. The company’s strength is that it can streamline a wide range of services such as sales automation, services, marketing, analytics, etc. all onto a dashboard. But can a dashboard compete with an AI assistant instantly summarizing every customer communication while providing all the information and data without the need (and expense) of a SaaS offering? Obviously, Salesforce is attempting to address these challenges with AI-integrating Agentforce, but the market has somewhat viewed these products as an attempt to hang onto its very predictable and profitable SaaS model, whereas AI-native platforms are built directly on top of Claude, ChatGPT, and open-source AI models not bound by stitched-together legacy software. As such, they can be more nimble while offering consumption-based pricing or an outcome-based model, like the one employed at 2024 start-up Sierra, whose co-founder is ex-Salesforce co-CEO Bret Taylor. Management is adjusting, offering Agentforce via subscription, consumption-based pricing, or hybrid.

That said, Salesforce isn’t succumbing to AI just yet, due to the size of its still growing customer base and the length of its contracts – typically one to three years. Furthermore, its incorporation of AI into its SaaS offerings that perform tasks on behalf of its clients makes them agentic and stickier. In other words, agentic AI isn’t replacing Salesforce, rather Salesforce is incorporating AI into its offerings to make its customers agentic. The company is its own customer zero, laying of ~4,000 support personnel due to Agentforce’s ability to handle routine inquiries and processes automatically. The flip side to all of this two-fold: first as agent bots replace humans, Salesforce’s subscription model encounters lower customer seat counts, necessitating the aforementioned pivot to consumption models; second, its incorporation of AI agents does not guarantee that current customers such as Anthropic won’t become competitors in the future.

4QFY26 Financials & FY27 Outlook

There appeared to be no ‘SaaS-pocalypse’ when the company reported 4QFY26 financials on February 25, 2026, posting earnings of $3.81 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $11.2 billion versus $2.78 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $10.0 billion in 4QFY25, representing gains of 37% and 12%, respectively. Subscription and support revenue (95% of total), rose 13% year-over-year to $10.7 billion. Current remaining performance obligations (CRPO – contracted revenue over the next 12 months) grew 16% versus the prior year period to $35.1 billion. However, that metric received a 3% boost from currency translations and a 4% boost from its acquisition of Informatica, meaning organic constant currency growth was 9%. Total RPO grew 14% year-over-year to a record $72.0 billion. In sum, Salesforce’s bottom line was $0.76 better than expectations while the top line was in line with consensus.

The company’s AI offerings, Agentforce and Data 360, produced annual recurring revenue [ARR] of $2.9 billion, up more than 200% from the prior year period and up over 100% year-over-year after removing the $1.1 billion contribution from Informatica. Agentforce ARR gained 169% to $800 million, as Salesforce continues to convert its agentic enterprise clientele. Over 60% of Agentforce and Data 360 bookings was derived from existing customers expanding commitments.

Growth at the total revenue line was Salesforce’s best showing on a year-over-year basis since 4QFY23 and brought FY26 to $12.52 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $41.5 billion versus $10.20 per share on total revenue of $37.9 billion in FY25, representing improvements of 23% and 10% (9% on a constant currency basis).

CEO Marc Benioff flashed his typical bold posture, stating that “If there is a ‘SaaS-pocalypse’, it may be eaten by ‘SaaS-quatch’… Anthropic runs its whole global operation on Salesforce and Slack. I think every AI company does.” He then conducted a Q&A with three Salesforce clients, who espoused the virtues of Agentforce and other company offerings.

More importantly, Salesforce provided an outlook for FY27, featuring non-GAAP earnings of $13.15 per share on revenue of $46.0 billion (based on range midpoints) for respective improvements of 5% and 11% as compared to FY26 – although the top-line guide included 3% for Informatica. Furthermore, management raised its FY30 revenue forecast from $60 billion to $63 billion, expressing confidence in its ability to grow at an 11% CAGR over the balance of the decade while achieving a Rule of 50 (operating margin plus revenue growth totaling at least 50%). It was 46.3 in 4QFY26.

Even with the significant beat at the bottom line, the sub-10% organic, constant currency CRPO and ongoing general skepticism regarding SaaS kept a cap on what could have been a better-received quarter, with shares of CRM up 4% to $199.47 in the subsequent trading session.

Balance Sheet and Analyst Commentary

Management’s bravado did not stop at its outlook. Benioff called the market’s treatment of his stock “not… rational” and to that end, Salesforce’s board authorized a $50 billion share repurchase program (replacing any outstanding ones), amounting to 28% of shares outstanding at its current price after executing $12.7 billion of buybacks in FY26. To prove its seriousness, the company raised $25 billion in a debt offering featuring interest rates between 4.5% and 6.7% and maturities from 2028 to 2066 on March 11, 2026 with the sole intent of repurchasing its own stock, not waiting for the $16.5 billion of expected free cash flow in FY27 to materialize. It also raised its quarterly dividend 6% to $0.44 a share for a current yield of 0.9%. Before the debt offering, Salesforce held cash and securities of $9.6 billion against debt of $10.4 billion on January 31, 2026.

One of the more widely covered stocks in the market, recent analyst firm coverage leans positive. On average, sell-side analysts expect Salesforce to earn $13.20 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $46.1 billion in FY27, followed by $14.93 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $50.6 billion in FY28.

In addition to authorizing an aggressive and sizable repurchase program, two of the company’s board members put their own money where their company’s money is earmarked, buying a total of 5,141 shares at an average price of $194.60 on March 18-19, 2026.

Verdict

Sentiment regarding SaaS may not change for some time, but with shares of CRM trading at just over 13 times FY27E non-GAAP EPS with relatively good visibility while management confidently projects Rule of 50 for FY30, bolstered by a $50 billion repurchase program, the downside seems somewhat truncated, making Salesforce a solid covered call candidate.

The options are quite liquid against this well-traded equity. Worries about AI disruption have also pushed the option premiums against CRM higher, meaning I can take a strike price nearly 10% under the current trading level of the stock for my covered call trade and still make a return in the mid-teens by yearend even if the stock goes down that 10%. To me, this is a better way to hold a small stake in Salesforce than straight equity given the current uncertainty around this space from AI disruption.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: Future Looks More Certain Than Before
data.description:
Introduction

Firefly Aerospace Inc. (FLY) is an interesting space company, which I have covered twice since its IPO, but both times I didn’t feel as though I myself wanted to start a position. On the IPO itself, the stock has been majorly overvalued, with a premium driven purely by momentum and sentiment, and later on, I wanted to see a more stable execution, breaking down the stock right after an explosion of Firefly’s first-stage booster for Alpha Flight 7 back in October. As a result of the unfortunate news and still elevated valuation multiples, I decided not to add the stock to my watchlist.

Back then, the stock traded slightly over $25 per share, going as low as $16 a couple of months after the coverage, which could, in theory, have been a more attractive entry point for me had I not missed it. Today, the stock has pulled back slightly from the rally it’s seen in the last couple of weeks, and earnings are coming in soon, which could be another positive catalyst for the stock price. In this article, I want to take a look at the latest news and quarterly earnings and to answer the question of what can happen with Firefly after they post the results.

Q4 2025

Looking at the latest earnings transcript, I was surprised to see that the management began by explaining what the company does. Usually, I take a look at well-established players, so maybe that’s the way to work with stocks that have just made their public offering. My dear readers can, of course, go and read my previous articles, where I break down this stock in detail, but I also want to add a management quote, as it shows what the company is truly focused on:

Firefly is a space and defense company, delivering innovative hardware and software to perform the hardest missions in the space for national security, exploration and commercial technology lighting the way to a bold space ecosystem that expands humanity's future… Our hardware is represented by 4 revenue-generating products, our small-lift Alpha rocket, medium-lift Eclipse rocket, Blue Ghost lander and Elytra satellite orbiter. Now with SciTec onboard, Firefly's capabilities include AI-enabled defense software proven in operations, such as missile warning and defense.

The management emphasized how the renewed interest in Moon missions, such as the recent Artemis, provides additional opportunities for Firefly and that their past success ensures they are perfectly positioned to benefit. Discussing past success, the company is referring to the Blue Ghost Mission 1, which launched January 15th, 2025, and successfully landed on the Moon March 2nd, 2025. The problem is, of course, that those projects take time, and even though the company highlights the fact that it has contracts for missions 2, 3, and 4 already, the second one is planned for late 2026.

The truly good news is that the company is now a part of the SHIELD contract, which makes this company a part of the Golden Dome project, which is effectively a system of satellites and space weapons that should cover the whole earth, allowing the country to detect and destroy various types of missiles, including ballistics. It is not only good news because the company could be getting money out of this deal, but also because the participation was made possible by the SciTech acquisition, which the company has just recently completed.

The news about the SciTech acquisition came out two days after my latest update, and I thought it might provide the company with the boost it needs to be better positioned for the governmental contracts. At the same time, the acquisition was relatively expensive, which, in my opinion, boosted the risk profile of FLY:

SciTec it’s a defense software and data analytics company that builds systems for missile tracking, surveillance, and space-domain awareness. In simpler terms, it does the software and intelligence work that is the "brain" of the hardware later on. Its technology processes huge streams of data and delivers this information to the military. By how it looks right now, it's a solid, recurring-revenue business that generated around $164 million over the past twelve months, and recently was awarded a $259 million Space Force contract.

This, by the way, is one of the reasons the company was able to present impressive growth in the recent earnings. Revenue growth has been exceptional at 541% YoY and 92% QoQ. It’s way easier for young companies, which had almost no sales before, to pull in growth rates like this than for the established players, but still, the market likes a good growth story and often awards those who stand up to that.

At the same time, the management confirmed the results were boosted by the SciTech acquisition, which added to the growth in the last 2 months of the quarter:

Now turning to our fourth quarter results, which include SciTec in the last 2 months of the year. This was the highest quarterly revenue in the company's history at $57.7 million. This compares with $30.8 million in the third quarter and $9 million in the same quarter a year ago. Within our total revenue, Spacecraft Solutions revenue was $50 million and launch revenue was $7.7 million. The sequential increase was primarily driven by completion of multiple milestones across the spacecraft business.

While this metric is strong, it will continue to be boosted by the SciTech recurring revenue streams, but that doesn’t solve all of the concerns of the market, because even with this nice addition, the company is deeply unprofitable. Firefly has an adjusted loss of about $57 million for this quarter. We could argue that it’s a natural phase, where the company invests a lot of money in building the tech, which should make money in the future, but it doesn’t mean it minimizes the risks. The company is paying for everything by itself, and if something goes wrong or the execution doesn’t prove to be successful, that could turn the balance sheet into a disaster. For most of the stocks, not ideal execution could mean a drag on momentum. Here, we could be talking about another explosion or crash, which usually takes a long time for the market to forget and forgive.

Adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter was a loss of $57.3 million compared with a loss of $46.3 million in the third quarter and a loss of $67.7 million in the fourth quarter a year ago. Turning to our balance sheet. We ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $893 million, which included $260 million drawn from our revolving credit facility… Free cash flow was a loss of $79.3 million compared with a loss of $62 million in the third quarter and a loss of $42.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. The change in free cash flow quarter-over-quarter is primarily driven by payments related to the acquisition and integration of SciTec.

At the same time, the company has a nice cash position, which adds confidence.

We ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $893 million, which included $260 million drawn from our revolving credit facility.

Another great sign is that the company has a growing backlog, which has just reached $1.4 billion, up 23% from a year ago. SciTech has added $170 million here, which means the company saw organic growth as well. Among the contracts that boosted this backlog are the Blue Ghost 4 for NASA, the VICTUS SOL Alpha launch from the Space Force, Elytra Mission 3 from DIU, the INCUS Alpha launch from NASA, and others.

As for the outlook, the management is confident that they will be able to get to $420-$450 million for the annual revenue, which would mean another year of triple-digit growth. Taking the midrange of $435 million, the management aims for 170% growth for 2026 from 2025. It looks like almost all of that revenue is already locked in, or at least, that’s what the CFO of the company has said on earnings:

I mean, as I mentioned to Sheila, out of our midpoint guidance number, 80% of that is already booked. So we feel pretty good about the guidance number. And I mean, as -- and as we ramp up the Alpha launches, that will stabilize that number.

On the upcoming earnings (May 4th), the most immediate number to watch out for would probably be revenue, as the market wants to see proof that the company is moving from the previous business model of launching the rocket and getting the revenue to a more predictable software business model, thanks to the SciTech acquisition. I would really like to see if there are any updates or any new additional information on the Golden Dome project, because if SciTech becomes one of the main software behind this, the company would likely re-rate even higher. Any positive news about the launch of Flight 8 of the Alpha Block 2 could serve as another positive catalyst. Talking about the financials, as with any unprofitable company, we have to keep an eye on cash and how the company manages it.

Valuation

Of course, the valuation is enormous. If we are looking at the EV-to-sales ratio, the stock trades at a 1300% premium compared to the sector. Investors are paying a massive premium for every past dollar generated. If we are looking forward, which accounts for the revenue prediction for the upcoming year, the stock’s premium goes down to about 470% above the sector. By any means, this stock isn’t cheap. At the same time, given the opportunities with the government and huge organizations, some premium is justified.

Conclusion

It is hard for me to put a Buy rating on this stock, even though I have to admit it looks much more compelling after the SciTech acquisition and everything that came together with it. It looks like the company is in its growth stage, rather than its development stage, which means we might have more catalysts. The fact that the company pivots from launch-only revenue to a more predictable software revenue is great, and it could provide the much-needed pillow for the risky part of the business. I rate FLY stock as a Buy this time around, although I would personally add it to my watchlist, hoping to catch it lower.

data.title: The Real Brokerage: Growth Is Real, But Value Capture Still Isn't
data.description:
Investment Summary

The Real Brokerage Inc. (REAX) has clearly built a real business and scaled agent count rapidly. It has taken share in a weak housing market with improved retention. Its cloud model seems to be structurally more efficient than office-based brokerage models.

The stock looks cheap at first sight, especially on an EV/Gross profit basis, but the question is not top-line growth alone. It is whether enough of that growth is actually retained for shareholders.

The problem is structural: REAX's fee structure that boosts growth also limits the business's monetization potential. The best agents are hitting annual caps, and revenue sharing with referrers is taking another significant chunk of remaining monetization. Stock-based compensation (SBC) is eating into a very large part of gross profits. At the same time, ancillary revenues like title, mortgage, and wallet are still too small to matter and offset the structural limitations of the brokerage business.

All-in-all, REAX is a business with clear strength but whose economics still seem too constrained to support a bullish view.

Business Overview

REAX is a cloud-based real estate brokerage platform operating in the US and Canada with a fully remote, agent-centric model that removes the need for traditional physical brokerage offices.

One of the main factors explaining REAX's success with agents is the fact that it has better economics for them than traditional brokerages, like attractive commission splits with annual caps and revenue sharing for referrals. It also provides SBC to its agents to incentivize them. Besides, REAX's fully digitalized platform removes the need for physical offices, replacing much of the traditional brokerage back office model.

REAX mostly monetizes transaction commissions, which are mostly pass-through. It is expanding in the broader transaction lifecycle and thereby also monetizing adjacent lines like mortgage, title, and fintech ("wallet") products, but these are still financially small. That said, there is indeed a logic to expand there, as the latter could eventually help improve margins and improve the engagement per agent.

What The Market Likes

Numbers displayed look solid at first sight. Q3/25 revenue was $568.5 million (+53%) with a gross profit of $44.9 million (+40%) and an adjusted EBITDA of $20.4 million with +54% YoY. In Q4/25, revenue was $505 million, up 44% YoY, with a gross profit of $39 million, up 30% YoY, with adjusted EBITDA of $14.2 million, up 56%. REAX improving its EBITDA and generating significant cash flow, despite being in a soft market, is indeed a strong feat.

Looking at the yearly picture, FY25 revenue was $1.97 billion (up 56% YoY) with a gross profit of $165.7 million (up 44% YoY) with operating expenses that were only up 25%. FY25 adjusted EBITDA landed at $62.9 million (+57%), with operating cash flow about $66 million.

On the agent side, REAX's cloud brokerage concept has shown it can recruit agents at scale despite not having the physical infrastructure of its peers. Since 2021, the agent count has grown over 8x. During Q3/25 and Q4/25, the growth in the number of transactions outpaced the broader housing market in a significant way. At the end of 2025, there were roughly 31.7 thousand, already over 33 thousand by early March.

This is impressive given that existing home sales were almost 40% lower in March 2026 compared to the January 2021 house sales peak in the US. REAX's retention seems better than the headline suggests. In Q3/25 and Q4/25, agent churn was 4.9% and 5.2%, but revenue churn was much lower at 1.4% and 1.6%, respectively, implying that the lower-producing agents are more likely to leave the platform.

Where The Story Breaks

The problem is, none of these positives necessarily translate into strong shareholder economics. Despite having a full cloud-based engine based on referrals for less sales-related/CapEx spend, margins are thin and are actually reducing YoY, showing that top-line growth is coming at a cost. Let's see why.

First, headline revenue growth overstates the potential of the business, as most of it is pass-through (a form of gross volume), with most revenue going back to agents, more so than peers, as REAX takes little commission compared to its legacy peers (15% vs. 30% often used for mid-sized traditional brokers). On top of that, another layer of fee sharing goes to referrers, who get another slice of the revenue. Agents will typically receive 85% of gross commission income, with REAX retaining 15% until the agent reaches the annual cap. After that cap is reached, the business starts earning a fixed fee per transaction, which means revenue from productive agents is becoming less monetizable on their marginal transactions. All these factors, whose purpose is to help recruit agents faster, are the same ones worsening the company's capacity to retain value.

Operating expenses, which include REAX's $60.5 million revenue share expense as well as part of agent equity compensation ($14.7 million), are scaling well (25%) relative to gross profit (44%) in 2025 relative to 2024. That said, the headline OpEx understates how much REAX's business model relies on financial incentives, since a large chunk of agent equity compensation is already accounted for in Cost of Sales ($43.7 million). Besides, revenue share still grew 42% YoY, which is broadly in line with gross profit. This shows how a significant portion of the cost structure remains directly tied to transaction activity.

This goes further looking at SBC and share dilution. Reported dilution looks somewhat partially contained, but the economic burden is still heavy. SBC looks manageable relative to revenue, but as we said above, it's mostly pass-through. Relative to gross profit, SBC appears massive, showing it consumes a very large part of the value that is actually generated by the business. The leakage for shareholders is significant. The business generates nice cash flow, but a very large portion of it is just used to offset the heavy SBC partially incurred to recruit agents. Indeed, most of the SBC is tied to agents (~$58.4 million) rather than employees and directors (~$9.7 million). This is significant because that large SBC is layered on top of all the revenue sharing and is completely tied to revenue growth. Management cannot easily stop it like with other tech company SBC programs without affecting REAX's growth engine itself.

Doing $39 million in buybacks this year is somewhat compensating for that effect. That said, a net YoY dilution of 4% is making the investment case much less appealing. Between 2020 and 2025, the share count went from roughly ~150 million to more than ~210.5 million shares, a ~40% increase. At the end of 2025, REAX also had approximately ~27.4 million shares reserved for RSUs and ~10.7 million shares reserved for options, showing that there is a much larger dilution overhang than the headline share count suggests. It's also noteworthy that REAX generates a lot of operating cash flow to afford those buybacks, but most of those cash flows are only used to offset the dilution that SBC brought in the first place.

Ancillary services, which could become nice contributors to the bottom line in the future, remain small economically speaking relative to the brokerage commission line generating $2 billion. In 2025, Title generated a revenue of $5.0 million. Mortgage generated $6.0 million, and Wallet generated $0.9 million. Altogether, these services generated $7.7 million of Gross Profit (GP), less than 5% of total GP. Importantly, they are all loss-making at the operating level as they scale (Title loses around $4.1 million, Mortgage loses around $2.2 million, and Wallet loses around $0.7 million at the operating level). This proves that these services are optional but not meaningful enough to offset the brokerage's model structural limitations.

Valuation

REAX is not expensive on an EV/Gross Profit basis. If gross profit quality improves over time with ancillary becoming meaningful and SBC being drastically reduced, then economics per share could improve and make the investment case more compelling, but that appears to still be a leap of faith for investors at this point. Its fee model is vulnerable to incentive leakage and equity compensation.

The question is not whether the stock seems cheap on the surface but whether it can actually start showing operating leverage and trickle down all the way to shareholders. For now, the business is showing operating leverage, but gross margins remain compressed due to a rising mix of capped transactions reducing marginal monetization at the gross profit level, with a large part of agent SBC embedded in the cost of sales. Operating profit is impacted by revenue share at the OpEx level, alongside the remaining portion of SBC recorded there. This further distributes REAX's retained economics at various layers of the income statement.

Company Model EV/GP Gross Margin GP Growth TTM
The Real Brokerage (REAX) Cloud brokerage ~3.2x ~8.4% +44%
eXp World Holdings, Inc. (EXPI) Cloud brokerage ~2.7x ~7.0% -2.5%
Compass, Inc. (COMP) Tech-enabled brokerage ~7.3x ~12.2% +28%

We chose the EV/Gross Profit (EV/GP) metric as it is more useful than EV/Sales for brokerage models that have large pass-through revenue. The Real Brokerage seems more or less in line with its closest peers with regard to valuation but manages to showcase some superior growth YoY, which needs to be put to its credit.

That said, this growth was partially subsidized by SBC, which resulted in much larger dilution than peers like eXp, which only faced a rough 11.6% dilution since 2020 (from 144 million to 161 million), showing a more disciplined approach to dilution despite operating a similar business model.

Conclusion

REAX has built a real business. Its platform is growing fast. It is taking share in a weak industry context. It improves retention. It has demonstrated that the cloud model works. But the fee structure that helps agent acquisition and retention also limits monetization. Gross margins are pressured, and ancillary is still too small to matter. Heavy SBC is really hurting real per-share compounding for retail investors. In terms of risk-reward, the current setup does not seem sufficiently compelling to justify a bullish stance.

data.title: Stocks Are Rising Despite Falling Liquidity Amid Mounting Risks
data.description:
What is turning out to be the first in a number of years is that the market has so far been able to rise following a sharp drain in liquidity around tax season. Historically, and as previously noted, risk assets have tended to struggle in the second half of April following tax day.

Reserve balances held at the Fed have fallen to about $2.9 trillion as of Thursday, April 23. The decline in reserves was due to the Treasury General Account rising, as expected, to around $1.04 trillion, up from about $690 billion at the end of March. This is a negative liquidity impulse, which historically has negative impacts on risk assets over time.

Overnight Rates Remain In Check

What has been noticeably different at present is that secured overnight financing rates have remained more stable, whereas interest on reserves. In the past, we have seen secured overnight financing rates spike, and spreads widened significantly. So while reserves have fallen, the pressure seen at the end of 2025 has not emerged at this point, and that could be a contributing factor to why risk assets have behaved much better than one would expect.

Collateral Levels Decline

However, it isn't only on the Fed balance sheet that liquidity issues appear to be evolving; secured overnight financing volumes have fallen dramatically, too. This actually started to take shape at the end of 2025 as well. Falling SOFR volumes may indicate less collateral in the overnight market. However, it could be related to multiple factors, such as a reduced need for financing or a contraction in primary dealer balance sheets. However, it could also be strongly linked to the reverse repo facility draining to near zero, with no further excess liquidity entering the overnight funding markets. In fact, the big rise in SOFR turnovers starts with the draining of the reverse repo facility, and the peak in SOFR volumes occurs when the reverse repo facility hits its lower bound.

So while liquidity levels have fallen at the Fed, we have also seen collateral availability decline in the overnight funding market. For the most part, we have yet to see a meaningful impact on equity repo financing activity, but there has been a mild decline more recently, one that warrants further monitoring. If rising collateral leads to increased repo financing, then one would think that falling collateral may then lead to further decline in financing.

Overall, it suggests that both liquidity levels and collateral availability in the market are declining and are not supporting the recent move higher in equity prices, implying that if the market is truly rising, it is doing so on its merits and without additional liquidity or collateral lending.

It leaves the recent rise in risk assets and the stock market disconnected from the trends seen in liquidity and collateral availability. Currently, price action in risk assets is surprising and historically an outlier, suggesting either that something is off and working in the background to offset this framework or that the equity markets' recent move is higher, running on fumes and merely waiting for the liquidity and collateral impulses to catch up.

data.title: LG Display: The Market Is Right To Be Nervous (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This would mark my second time covering LG Display Co., Ltd., (LPL), a company you probably know, and whose screens might even be in your home. Previously, I gave it a “cautious" Buy rating. Regarding that caution, I wrote:

I realize that making a 'buy' call as the market shows a second straight day of triple-digit losses, especially in tech, comes with a very high risk of softer earnings in 2026 and the usual global headwinds. But risk is risk, and I'm not taking a big position here, just basing it on solid OLED execution that could make it worth it over the longer term.

Technically speaking, since my Buy call, on at least two separate occasions it looked like the stock was really ready to break out and, more important, hold that momentum. But so far, at best, it’s performed more like something you’d swing trade, not buy and hold for the long term. Now, after reviewing the latest earnings after this selloff, I’m downgrading the stock to neutral. OLED is turning for the better, but the recovery is still too uneven to justify another fresh buy call right now.

My Take on LG Display’s Latest Quarter

  • Revenue: KRW 5.534 trillion
  • Revenue YoY: -9%
  • Revenue QoQ: -23%.

Revenue dropped compared to last year. Two things caused that. The initial factor is that the first quarter of each year is typically a slow time for TV and phone sales. So every year, like clockwork, shipments drop in the first quarter once the holiday buying binge is over. The second factor contributing to the decrease in revenue was LG Display’s decision to no longer offer LCD TV panel sales in the first quarter of 2025. That product line wasn't making much money anyway.

  • Operating Profit: KRW 146.7 billion
  • Operating Margin: 3%
  • EBITDA Margin: 21%
  • OLED Share of Revenue: 60% (+5pp YoY)
  • ASP per m: $1,244
  • ASP YoY: +55%.

LG Display has now made an operating profit three quarters in a row, including this past Q1, which is historically the weakest quarter of the year. It happened because OLED panels now make up 60% of total revenue, up from a year ago. OLED panels are more expensive and more profitable than the old LCD stuff. LG Display’s price per square meter rose year-over-year, driven by changes in its product lineup.

  • ASP QoQ: -4%.

Sequentially (comparing to Q4 2024), average selling prices for panels did decline, but this decline is due to mobile phone panels having higher per-unit prices, even though they are smaller. Seasonal mobile shipment declines during Q1 contribute to this overall decline in the blended average of all products. That's just math, not a sign that customers are pushing back on prices.

  • Net Income: -KRW 575.7 billion.

LG Display had an operating profit but reported a net loss due to losses from foreign currency translations. LG Display carries a significant chunk of its debt in foreign currencies, and the Korean won has been weak. When the won devalues compared to these foreign currencies, the value of the debt increases when expressed in won; that creates a reported loss even when operations are making money. Essentially, a weak won and high-rate environment makes it harder for LG Display to pay down debt and generate free cash flow. It could be worth watching.

The demand picture has a wrinkle: management said customers were pulling orders forward. Customers ordered sooner than they normally would due to concerns about rising prices for memory chips. There are two ways to view the impact. First, current quarter demand looks good, but second, current quarter demand is actually borrowed from future quarters. In other words, the strong demand we are seeing today may be demand moved from Q2 and Q3 and therefore is not indicative of future demand.

It appears external factors pose a threat to the market. Management identified several risks that could impact sales performance: semiconductor shortages, rising commodity prices, and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Additionally, competitors such as Samsung Display and BOE are pushing harder into OLED displays. There are also Chinese companies building large, newer-generation factories (gen-8 and gen-9) that could start exerting pricing pressure on mid-tier display products. Micro-LED technology may pose a long-term threat to OLED's dominance as well.

  • OLED Monitor Share of Shipments: ~20% (from low teens).

Here's where LG Display has the wind at its back. It has two key product categories where there is growing demand: OLED gaming monitors and Apple Watch display panels. OLED gaming monitors are experiencing strong growth; management believes that the category will account for roughly 20% of large-panel shipments this year, compared to a low-teen percentage previously. The bigger story is with Apple Watch display panels, where LG Display is now effectively the sole supplier after Japan Display’s exit from the segment. Analysts are also predicting accelerating earnings from these products for the remainder of the year.

  • New OLED Investment: ~KRW 1.1 trillion
  • CapEx 2026: ~KRW 2 trillion.

In the meantime, LG Display committed roughly one trillion Korean won in fresh capital investment in OLED technology, within their already-announced full-year capital expenditure budget. Also, on foldable phones, management said they have experience making midsized foldable panels and are watching the smartphone foldable market closely. But they won't commit production capacity until they can see demand more clearly.

Lastly, the restructuring program is still a drag. For the second year running, LG Display ran a voluntary retirement program, paying employees to leave as part of a broader cost reduction and restructuring effort. That adds real costs in the short term and, as management admitted, is creating shareholder "fatigue." They said this should be the last time they need to do it.

LPL's Valuation

Starting first with Seeking Alpha's valuation data, we can see an EV/EBITDA of 4.36x trailing and 3.83x forward against a sector median above 19x. These are profound discounts. So my initial read here is that the market's pricing in either a reversion in profitability or a structural ceiling on earnings quality.

In the meantime, price to sales at 0.29x trailing, against a sector median above 3x, seems to repeat the same thing.

Switching to FAST Graphs for context, we have a blended price to earnings of 19.56x, well above the normal P/E of 16.42x, which signals a contrarian take here that LPL's not cheap on a normalized earnings basis despite the sector-relative discount. Yet, estimated fair value's about $14, while the current price is around $4, meaning about a 250% upside. But we have to remember that that gap depends entirely on the earnings recovery materializing on schedule.

That noted, the 2026 forward P/E is not meaningful, and the 2027 estimate of 40x implies consensus still expects thin earnings two years out.

Revenue is declining year-over-year and forward, earning D and F grades respectively. EBITDA growth is positive but decelerating.

The bright spot is free cash flow, where levered FCF growth is exceptional and forward operating cash flow growth grades a B, suggesting the capital restructuring is beginning to generate real cash.

Technically, after yesterday's 20% single-session decline, price has now broken below all key moving averages, which typically signals capitulation but can also precede continued selling if sentiment deteriorates further.

That said, RSI at 47 is not oversold. At least from my angle, there's no technical floor being signaled here.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m downgrading LPL to a Hold. There’s upside based on the potential for improving OLED earnings and free cash flow, but I think the downside risk is heavier because of declining revenue, FX-sensitive risk tied to its leveraged balance sheet, a challenging restructuring effort, and a stock price that has lost all technical support. I’m going to hold my position for now, but if you’re just kicking the tires here for the first time, I’d only buy if you’re a patient, risk-tolerant investor comfortable with turnaround volatility.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: No Growth, Consumer Headwinds, And Depleting Margins
data.description:
The global consumer packaged food company, Kraft Heinz (KHC), is down 27% in the last year and is gearing up to post Q1 earnings in early May. This large cap consumer staple firm is best known for brands like Heinz ketchup, Kraft cheese and mac & cheese, Oscar Mayer, and Philadelphia cream cheese. Majority of their revenue comes from North American grocery retail and the rest from international and foodservice channels. I gave the stock a sell rating less November on changing consumer habits and rough management outlook. The stock is down 12% since that time.

The packaged food industry continues to face structural headwinds, as consumers shift more to private-label grocery items. Pricing power is becoming a challenge for KHC as core demand shrinks and margins contract. Management is moving into 2026 with a new plan in attempts to fix profits, but I think the tough sledding is still in early stages and still have KHC as a sell.

Steady Volume Declines

KHC has seen volume decline in 15 of its last 16 quarters, with sales volumes dropping by 3.8% in the last two years. This has to be a huge concern, as were not talking just a few quarters of regress here, were talking almost half a decade. If you look at Seeking Alpha’s analyst revisions on future revenue growth, it is difficult to see a light at the end of the tunnel. Every single analyst has downward revisions on earnings and revenue, and annualized revenue growth over the next half decade is about 1%.

Management is pinning this on reduced demand for processed and packaged food, consumers looking toward private-label brands, and worsening volume elasticity on price increases. KHC is expecting organic net sales to drop 1.5%-3.5% in 2026, based on their outlook provided earlier this year. Their YoY revenue has declined in 9 straight quarters, and I suspect it will be the same in the upcoming Q1 report. It could be their first quarter with under $6 billion in revenue in more than 5 years.

Margin Trouble

KHC gross margin has remained fairly stable over time, hovering around 32%, which is about where they sit now, which is right in line with the sector. Where they are getting smacked is with their operating margin, which is down 25.2% in the last year. Operating income was down about 15% from year prior. The pressure on operating margin from volume declines and weak pricing power has been noticeable, and based on the current managerial plan for 2026, I think could get even worse.

Moving Forward?

KHC management has laid out a clear plan in attempts to get the company back on track. The plan is not a cost cutting method, but rather a goal of turning demand around for their product and market. They are investing $600 million across four key areas: marketing, research and development, sales and promotions, and product improvement. They have been losing market share for a decade in the U.S. and are seeing their largest revenue region deplete further and further.

They outline that it will be a 20% boost in R&D and an attack plan to win market share in brands like Capri Sun and Lunchables, along with taste elevation brands like Heinz and Philadelphia.

I am not going to sit here and say this plan couldn’t work in the long run, and in fact I do think that a big changeup was needed. But this does mean that this type of spend will lead to sacrificing near term profits to try to fix long-term growth. Operating margin will continue to sink while they work through this investment period. They have already noted they expect adjusted operating income to be roughly 16% lower this year. Management expects to return to growth mode in 2027, but for me I think it will take much longer than that, especially considering industry headwinds, which is a key component as to why I am keeping my sell rating.

Headwinds Are Real

About 80% of KHC brands are associated with highly processed foods. I think most everyone has noticed that there is a shift in consumer habits and spending in the food industry. The food pyramid has been flipped upside down, and Americans seem to be prioritizing healthier eating and habits more than they have in decades. This is not good news for KHC. 64% of consumers say that they limit processed foods and 64% say they look in more depth at food labels than they used to.

KHC is making efforts to revamp some product. For example, a Capri Sun “Hydrate” kids drink with electrolytes. I think it is great they are trying on newer product, but I don’t believe it will move the needle enough and think that headwinds in this space are going to delay their investment plan to return to growth.

Risk & Expectations

The biggest risk to my thesis is that they have a forward PE ratio of 10.7x, which is well below the industry median. I think that valuation alone isn’t a fair assessment for KHC, and that the low volume growth is the true tell-tale here. They are soon expected to report an adjusted EPS of $0.50 for Q1, which would be 19% lower YoY. Lack of earnings growth now and over the next year or two during this “development” period is going to make the stock’s value look much less discounted soon.

Another risk I will note is the recent deal KHC finalized with the NFL on becoming the leagues first ever condiment partner. This aligns with their marketing play, and will not directly drive up revenue, but is sure to gain momentum on eyes seeing their product as the U.S. has over 200 million football fans.

In next week's earnings, I think it will be most important to see management tone on consumer habits and spending trends on their product. I think we know based on the development plan, that operating margins are inevitable going to struggle for the time being. More importantly for KHC, is the direction the industry heads and comments on how management believes the $600 million spending plan has been so far.

Conclusion

One other piece to note is the massive non-cash impairment charge of $9.3 billion after weaker growth expectations and a sustained drop in its market value forced it to write down key brand assets. This won't impact cash flow directly, but it’s a warning sign that the company’s long-term earnings power may be weaker than previously thought, which also helps justify their lower valuation. I don't love this stock in the near-term, and issues like this also make the long-term outlook bleak too.

The consistent volume declines, direction operating margin is heading, and heavy industry headwinds are just too much for KHC right now. This won’t be the year for this stock, and I have KHC as a sell. Check back in toward the end of the year to see if the research and marketing plan has had any effect yet.

data.title: United Microelectronics Q1 Preview: Not Very Exciting (Downgrade)
data.description:
I sold my United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) position back in June of last year for a scratch, and I haven’t been following its ascent since then. So, now that it is due to report on the 29th of April, I thought it would be a good time to revisit. The company’s performance has been lagging most of its peers, and I don’t find it particularly interesting to jump back in.

Last time I covered it, I was sure it will pay out, and eventually it did, but I deployed the money into something I had a lot more conviction in, which paid off in the end.

The performance

Starting from the top, we can see that the company’s foundry services weren’t particularly in demand, as compared to other companies, which may be more involved in advanced nodes rather than mature nodes that UMC specializes in. We can see one standout quarter mid-2025, when sales jumped from $7B to $8B, which only then settled down in the following quarters. Shipments jumped during that period, and the node mix improved. Also, end-market demand recovered in communications and certain consumer/industrial areas after the 2023-2024 inventory digestion.

Let’s take a look at the company’s profitability and efficiency next. Overall, we can see a negative trend in the last two years of operations. It seems that the demand for the company saw for its foundries was not sufficient to employ the operating leverage to the max. Foundries have a lot of fixed costs, and if utilization rates are low due to soft demand, the produced units cannot be spread efficiently across all the costs, leading to a decline in profitability across the board.

This is also not what you’re looking for in a semiconductor company. The semiconductor business has been booming, with ridiculous demand for AI and data centers, but it seems that was not the case for UMC.

With the decline in the company’s bottom line, we can see that its other key efficiency metrics followed suit. These have become a lot less attractive, and some even went under my minimum requirements for a good investment. Clearly, with the softer demand that led to underutilization, the management is not using the company’s assets as one would prefer, and the use of shareholder capital is not as efficient as it was. ROTC was already under 10%, which is the minimum I like to see from an investment, and is close to 6% now. The company seems to be losing its moat and pricing power, and that is not ideal.

Looking at the company’s financial situation, UMC finished the year with around $4B in cash and equivalents against $1.45B in long-term debt. This is a great position to be in. Many, if not all, semiconductor companies that I cover have amazing liquidity that helps them get through the downcycles when they do happen, as was the case back in early 2024. Currently, I’m seeing lackluster performance from UMC, and I would call it a downturn since profitability has declined, but the mountain of cash is going to help it survive it and push through. The company’s cash flow generation ability has been very strong. It generated over $1B in cash from operations, and even after $427m in capex, it had around $579m in free cash flow.

Overall, I did not like how the company’s operations progressed over the last two years. The top-line growth was lacking, and because of that, profitability took a hit, as well as its pricing power and moat. The only positive out of the whole situation is the company’s ability to generate a decent amount of cash and its formidable balance sheet. I’m honestly surprised that the company’s share price is up over 76% for the year and 55% YTD.

Upcoming Quarter

Analysts are expecting the company to make $0.13 in EPS on $1.92B in sales. This translates to around 2% decline sequentially, and around 10% growth y/y. A respectable annual growth, but nothing impressive. Profitability remains the same as well.

Unfortunately, the management doesn’t provide concrete numbers for the upcoming quarter, although they did say they believe the company will see flat wafer shipments, ASP in U.S. dollars firm, and gross margins in the high 20% range. Also, capacity utilization is going to be in the mid-70% range, which signals that the company is not tight on capacity, so an increase in CapEx to $1.5B seems overdone.

Over the last ten quarters, the company beat revenue estimates 80% of the time, while the earnings picture was a lot more mixed, with a lot of misses in the recent quarters.

So, do I think the company is going to beat or miss this time around? It’s hard to tell honestly, but I want to say the company’s performance is underestimated in terms of the bottom and top lines, but I think further margin deterioration will bring its earnings below estimates.

Comments on the Outlook

Being a more specialized/mature-node player, people shouldn’t be expecting the same performance that one of my holdings currently experiences, and that is, of course, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). TSMC is aggressively building out capacity because of the ridiculous demand for cutting-edge advanced chips for AI data centers. The company’s advanced-node capacity has already been taken up by reservations, with many customers already pre-booking capacity into 2027. This drives up its utilization rates, which then support its amazing margin profile, which doesn’t even look like they belong to a manufacturing company, but more like a software company. That is because the advanced chips carry much better unit economics compared to mature nodes that UMC produces, with a big chunk of the revenues coming from the “golden nodes” as they call it in the 22/28nm processes.

I would like to see UMC’s utilization rates increase closer to high-80s- low-90s; otherwise, I’m afraid it will continue to lose profitability. I haven’t found any information on whether the company will see a boost in sales going forward. The guidance doesn’t support this view right now, so there was essentially no reason for the share price to be up over 70% in one year, apart from the fact that the company’s share price was already depressed, but with deteriorating profitability, I don’t think that was the case.

The management needs to work on revitalizing the company’s top-line growth because the current environment doesn’t support healthy growth in profitability.

Should you buy before earnings?

I think there is no reason to jump in right now. The company’s share price is up over 55% YTD, which seems unusual for a company that does not seem to be doing particularly well. The demand for its products is not there because all eyes are on advanced node processes like the ones that TSMC is an expert at.

Even if the mature nodes, like the ones that UMC produces, specifically 28nm and above, are still in demand, it doesn’t seem to be very profitable if the company’s profitability keeps grinding down. They are still the backbone in the auto, industrial, and consumer end-markets, but the fact that these aren’t growing as much as AI data centers means they are not as attractive an investment for now.

I already sold my small position that I had, and I am not looking to buy back in at these prices; however, if you are still an owner of UMC, I don’t see a point in selling. I think semiconductor companies will continue to experience growth in share price, mainly because all of them are associated with AI, even if they may not be producing anything significantly close to AI applications. If you already held through the lows, you might as well keep holding and see where it goes.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Toast: The Profit Era Begins
data.description:
It appears that Toast (TOST) is viewed as yet another victim of the software apocalypse. I view that fear as being misplaced, as the hardware and high switching costs are arguably more important factors. TOST has historically been a consolidator in a fragmented POS market and I expect that to continue moving forward. The company has demonstrated impressive profitability improvements, culminating in robust GAAP profit margins which I expect to expand meaningfully moving forward. It is time to upgrade the stock to a strong buy rating.

TOST Stock Price

I last covered TOST in July where I rated it a buy in a comparison article with Block (XYZ). In that article, I did prefer XYZ, which has gained slightly since, though TOST has been absolutely crushed.

The steep multiple contraction at TOST has de-risked the only problem that I saw in the past: valuation.

TOST Stock Key Metrics

I view TOST to be a best-in-class financial technology company focused on catering to restaurants. This has historically been a highly fragmented space. On the one hand this would imply a lot of competition, but TOST appears to be the shark in the room. Restaurants may not be so eager to switch away from existing POS systems due to significant switching costs but when they do, they typically move to providers like TOST as evidenced by the company’s growing market share.

In the most recent quarter, TOST generated strong results, with gross payment volume (‘GPV’) jumping 22% YoY to $51.4 billion. For reference, XYZ saw its Square GPV jump by only 10% YOY to $65 billion in its most recent quarter. The fact that TOST is able to generate double the growth rate at nearly the same scale is a clear indication that TOST is growing market share at an accelerated pace.

The company also made great strides in terms of profitability. The company was solidly profitable on a GAAP basis for both the quarter and full-year. I note that the company generated an impressive 59% and 68.5% incremental GAAP operating margin in the fourth quarter and full-year, respectively. I reference incremental margins to get an idea for where profit margins are trending over the long term.

Management has previously targeted 40% EBITDA margins over the long term (based on gross profits) and essentially achieved that in 2025. I note that the company generated similar incremental EBITDA margins for the quarter and year as well.

TOST ended the quarter with $2 billion of cash and investments, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around 20% growth in the first quarter and full-year. With earnings coming up on May 7th, I expect the guidance for adjusted EBITDA to prove woefully conservative given that they imply considerable margin degradation even as I expect consistent and robust margin expansion moving forward. That said, it is possible for the top-line to experience some volatility given the ongoing uncertainty from macro circumstances such as the Iran conflict.

On the conference call, management noted that they “now power 20% of SMB and mid-market restaurants in the U.S.” which is double over the last 3 years. TOST hasn’t been crushed as much as many software stocks, but it is possible that some of the recent weakness is due to similar fears of AI disruption. Management dispelled such fears, calling AI “an opportunity for Toast to lean in.” Management believes that their AI offering ToastIQ will further their positioning as the restaurants’ end-to-end operating system. I find it unlikely for AI to directly disrupt the Toast ecosystem given the hardware component. Instead, I expect Toast to be able to drive accelerated growth from offering AI products to their growing and expansive customer base, making TOST a long term winner in AI.

Is TOST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

After the stock’s struggles over the last few quarters, the valuation premium that it previously enjoyed has all but vanished. TOST recently traded at just over 20x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for significant operating leverage, powered by near 20% top-line growth over the coming years.

In my prior report, I noted the premium valuation but outlined a path to upside based on the company eventually becoming a payment processor themselves, leading to structurally higher gross margins. But after the valuation compression, the stock looks compelling even without needing to make such assumptions. I have mentioned that the company generated roughly 60% incremental GAAP operating margins (based on gross profits) in the recent quarter and past year. The stock trades at just under 10x forward gross profits. Assuming a 50% GAAP net margin over the long term, that would place the valuation at around 20x long term earnings. I view that 50% assumption to be appropriate given the strong incremental margins as well as my view (certainly not a unique one) that AI should allow for TOST to realize significant cost savings. If TOST was able to achieve such impressive incremental margins with how AI was over the past year, how high could margins could get in light of recent and future improvements in the AI models? My guess is that my 50% assumption ends up being too conservative. Even then, that places the stock at right around a 1x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). Typically that is enough to call the stock cheap, but it is worth discussing why I view TOST to be insulated from AI disruption risks. While the company’s moat appears to be largely back-end software, none of that software would work without the POS in place. I find it highly unlikely for restaurants to move away from TOST unless competing platforms are exponentially better. If that were not the case, then I would have expected TOST to have posted even stronger growth in past years (an indication that simply having the better product is not always enough to get restaurants to switch POS providers). As a result, I expect TOST to continue on its growth trajectory. While I can see the stock re-rating to a 1.5x PEG ratio, even sustaining that 1x PEG ratio may allow for around 18% to 20% annual returns in-line with the growth profile.

TOST Stock Risks

It is possible that pricing competition intensifies, perhaps due to AI. It is possible that AI enables competitors to quickly catch up in terms of comprehensive offerings, which would commoditize the playing field. It is possible that the economy sours and TOST experiences slowing GPV growth. It is possible that more and more transactions take place on delivery services like Doordash (DASH) which might post a surprising and indirect threat.

TOST Stock Conclusion

It can feel dangerous to pick winners in the software apocalypse, but valuations have fallen so much that it might be warranted. TOST looks like one such winner, backed by a strong balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and best-in-class product offering. TOST is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stock Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy rating
